data.title: New Rio Tinto CEO pledges cost cuts, asset sales in restructuring to boost profits
data.description:
Rio Tinto (RIO) -1.6% in early trading Thursday after new CEO Simon Trott outlined a plan to cut costs and sell assets in an effort to make the business "stronger, sharper and simpler" by delivering $5 billion-$10 billion in cash proceeds from its asset base by divesting, selling minority stakes, cutting roles, and restructuring some existing financing.

"We are delivering strong early productivity benefits and cost savings with more to come," having already taken actions to deliver $650 million in annualized productivity gains, Trott said at Rio's (RIO) first capital markets day since taking the helm in August. 

Rio (RIO) said it would throttle back lithium operations to only existing lithium projects, a move that was widely expected by investors.

The company already had said it would put its titanium and borates businesses up for sale, and "we will proceed and test the market for the assets, together with some of the other measures across our footprint - things like land, infrastructure, processing assets, and mining assets... so we're reaching up to $10 billion," Trott said.

J.P. Morgan analysts said the update contained no major strategic shifts, and analysts at RBC said the updated strategy was more conservative then peer Glencore, which promised a Mount Everest of copper production earlier this week.

Citi analysts were more optimistic, saying Rio (RIO) "has laid out an attractive vision for the company, with positive guidance commentary for 2025 and 2026, reiterating the solid volume growth outlook by 2030 and capex normalization."

Rio (RIO) also raised its 2025 copper production forecast, citing ramped up operations at its Oyu Tolgoi project in Mongolia, now seeing copper production this year of 860,000-875,000 metric tons on a consolidated basis, up from its previous forecast of 780,000-850,000 tons, while anticipating 2026 copper output of 800,000-870,000 tons.

The company earns most of its profit from iron ore but it is shifting focus towards copper, aiming to produce 1 million tons/year of the metal by 2030.

data.title: BMO Q4 earnings climb past consensus on capital markets, U.S. banking strength
data.description:
Bank of Montreal's (BMO) fiscal Q4 earnings, released Thursday, came in stronger than expected as noninterest income climbed, while its credit loss provision retreated. It's capital markets and U.S. banking units recorded notable gains.

Q4 adjusted EPS of C$3.28, exceeding the C$3.03 average analyst estimate, rose from C$3.23 in Q3 and C41.90 a year earlier.

Adjusted revenue of C$9.34B in the quarter ended Oct. 31, 2025, also surpassing the C$8.96B consensus, advanced from C$8.99B in the three months ended July 31, 2025, and C$8.96B in the year-ago period.

BMO shares dipped 1.4% in Thursday morning trading.

Adjusted net interest income was unchanged sequentially at C$5.50B, but increased from C$4.85B a year before.

Adjusted noninterest revenue was C$3.85B, up from C$3.49B in Q3 and C$3.52B in Q4 2024.

Provision for credit losses fell to C$755M in Q4 from C$797M in the prior quarter and C$1.52B in Q4 2024.

Adjusted noninterest expense of C$6.29B compared with C$5.01B in Q3 and C$4.88B in Q4 2024.

Results by segment:

  • Canadian Personal & Commercial Banking adjusted net income rose 5% Y/Y to C$800M 
  • U.S. Banking adjusted net income of C$871M surged by C$518M Y/Y.
  • Wealth Management adjusted net income of C$384M gained 28% Y/Y.
  • Capital Markets adjusted net income of C$532M jumped 97% Y/Y.

For fiscal 2025, "we delivered both robust earnings growth and improved return on equity, driven by significant pre-provision, pre-tax earnings expansion, and sustained positive operating leverage," BMO Financial Group CEO Darryl White said in a statement.

data.title: KALA BIO to raise $10 mln in registered direct offering
data.description:
• KALA BIO (NASDAQ:KALA) on Thursday said that it has entered into definitive agreements for the purchase of an aggregate of 10,000,000 shares of its common stock, at  $1.00 per share in a registered direct offering priced at-the-market under Nasdaq rules.
  • The closing of the offering is expected to occur on or about December 5, 2025, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.
  • The aggregate gross proceeds are expected to be approximately $10 million and the company currently intends to use the net proceeds to use the funds to repay debt and for general corporate purposes.
  • KALA is +14.59% to $1.06.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Delta, United, American airlines set to embark on a 'supermajor super cycle' -- Citi Research
data.description:
The airline industry’s underperformance in 2025 and capacity cuts have resulted in a “tactically bullish cyclical setup” for 2026, predominantly for those that Citi Research calls the “supermajors,” anticipating that their outperformance will prove more durable and persistent than even the most bullish expect.

Often referred to as legacy carriers, Citi’s John Godyn labels American Airlines (AAL), Delta Air Lines (DAL), and United Airlines (UAL) as “supermajors,” those airlines that have “successfully wrapped a new style of travel business model around a unique set of assets that no existing competitive or new entrant can easily recreate.”

As the supermajors have shown, this business model has enabled them to outperform both during downturns and upturns.

By contrast, legacy low-cost carriers ((LLCC)) are those whose business models were largely formed –and finely turned -- under conditions and assumptions that no longer characterize the market today, catering to an entire category of traveler that based their choice strictly on low fares and no frills.

However, as the market became saturated with this easily copied business model, and as airlines are no longer able to stimulate demand through lower fares, LLCCs are now restructuring with turnaround plans that borrow heavily from supermajors but without the “differentiated set of assets that the supermajors have benefited from after decades of investment.”

As such, this anticipated “supermajor super cycle,” will amplify the wedge that has formed since COVID between the supermajors and the LLCCs in favor of the supermajors.

Or rather, Godyn believes “investors are likely to be surprised at the upside leverage that the supermajors will have to a reaccelerating cycle despite the fact that most of the supermajors have outperformed dramatically already throughout the airline downturn of late.”

Of the three Buy-rated airlines (UAL, AAL, DAL), Delta (DAL) is the only airline that Godyn has removed his High Risk rating, a “strong reflection of the durability and consistency of its strategy over time.”

United (UAL), Godyn predicts, will exceed its 2019 peak EPS by the largest amount of all the major carriers by leaning into its international strength, while American (AAL) offers the most significant upside potential of the three if it is able to demonstrate strong execution of its effort to regain corporate and premium travel market share.

And while Alaska Air Group (ALK) is not technically a supermajor, Godyn rates it a Buy as well as management’s strategic plan for the carrier is “clearly designed to further develop its supermajor-like strengths.”

Meanwhile, JetBlue (JBLU) and Southwest (LUV) are two names trying to reinvent their LLCC business model in various legacy airline-like ways. For JetBlue (JBLU), the “JetForward” plan could result in meaningful upside in shares, but until there is some noticeable impact in JBLU’s results, Godyn retains his Sell/High Rist rating on the stock.

For Southwest (LUV), Godyn does not approach the carrier’s turnaround plan with the same level of skepticism as JetBlue (JBLU) given the presence of a vocal activist investor and gives Southwest (LUV) a Neutral/High Risk rating. However, Southwest’s (LUV) embrace of a supermajor-like playbook without the benefit of supermajor advantages creates an “unusual degree of brand/customer dissonance and execution risk.”

data.title: Paramount Skydance questions fairness of Warner Bros. sales process - report
data.description:
Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is questioning the "fairness and adequacy" of the Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) sales process. 

Paramount Skydance (PSKY) said Warner Bros. (WBD) isn't being fair in its sales process and isn't acting in the best interest of shareholders, according to a letter attorneys for Paramount wrote to the Warner Bros. board, CNBC reported.  In particular, WBD appears to be favoring a Netflix bid over several offers from Paramount. 

“It has become increasingly clear, through media reporting and otherwise, that WBD appears to have abandoned the semblance and reality of a fair transaction process, thereby abdicating its duties to stockholders, and embarked on a myopic process with a predetermined outcome that favors a single bidder,” according to the letter.

CNBC's David Faber earlier reported that Netflix (NFLX) is the leading bidder  for Warner Bros. (WBD) with a bid composed of 85% cash and 15% stock. 

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) told CNBC it confirmed to Paramount (PSKY) that it had received the letter and it plans to share it with members of the WBD board.

“Please be assured that the WBD Board attends to its fiduciary obligations with the utmost care, and that they have fully and robustly complied with them and will continue to do so,” the company said in its response to Paramount.

Shares of Netflix (NFLX) and Warner Bros. (WBD) both dropped 1.4%, while Paramount Skydance (PSKY) fell 1.7%.

data.title: The New York Times takes Pentagon to court over new press policy
data.description:
The New York Times (NYT) said Thursday it is suing the Pentagon after it implemented a new policy on U.S. military reporting, which the newspaper alleges violates its journalists' First and Fifth Amendment rights.

The newspaper claimed its journalists are at risk of losing their "Pentagon Facilities Alternate Credential" if they publish anything that has not been approved by the government officials under the new policy.

The 41-page lawsuit claims that the Pentagon's new policy will deprive the public of vital information about the American military and its leadership, and the policy seeks to restrict journalists' ability "to ask questions of government employees and gather information to report stories that take the public beyond official pronouncements."

"The Pentagon has made clear that lawful, routine newsgathering techniques—asking questions of government employees and interviewing them for stories—whether on or off Pentagon grounds could, in the Department's view,  "constitute a solicitation that could lead to revocation" of their PFACs," the lawsuit said.

The lawsuit notably includes War Secretary Pete Hegseth and Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell as defendants and NYT's U.S. intelligence agencies reporter Julian Barnes as a plaintiff. The case is The New York Times Company v. Department of Defense, Civil Case No. 25-4218, filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia.

Earlier this year, major news outlets, including NYT, Reuters (NASDAQ:TRI), The Wall Street Journal (NASDAQ:NWS) (NASDAQ:NWSA), Associated Press, Bloomberg News, The Washington Post, CNN (NASDAQ:WBD), Fox News (NASDAQ:FOX) (NASDAQ:FOXA), CBS (NASDAQ:PSKY), NBC (NASDAQ:CMCSA), ABC (NYSE:DIS), NPR, Axios, Politico, The Guardian, The Atlantic, and Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX), objected to the Pentagon's press policy.

News outlets argued that the Pentagon's new policy threatened to criminalize national security reporting and restrict the gathering of even unclassified information not pre-approved for release.

So far, only one major national news outlet, One America News Network, has publicly signed the new press policy.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Poll finds 25% could drop ACA insurance coverage if enhanced subsidies not extended
data.description:
• A new poll from the Kaiser Family Foundation found that a significant percentage of individuals who currently receive their health insurance through Affordable Care Act health insurance exchanges would "very likely" drop coverage if enhanced premium subsidies are not extended into 2026.
  • An analysis from KFF released in late September found that ACA marketplace premiums would more than double without an expansion of enhanced tax credits. If that were to happen, 25% of enrollees said they would probably go without insurance. Another one-third said they would look for a lower-cost plan, which typically have higher deductibles and co-pays.
  • The poll also found that about 60% wouldn't be able to shoulder an increase of $300 per year in the amount without a significant impact on household finances. Another 20% said they couldn't afford a $1,000 per year increase.
  • If overall healthcare costs rise by $1,000 in 2026, two-thirds of enrollees said they would likely cut spending on daily household needs, while 54% would likely to try to find another job or work extra hours, and a third would take out a loan or take on credit card debt.

data.title: Swiss arms export rules to loosen, boosting defense sector
data.description:
Switzerland is moving to ease its restrictive regulations on weapons exports, a change that provides a boost to its domestic defense industry, which has been struggling since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Lawmakers advanced the measure on Thursday, setting the stage for a final parliamentary vote on the amendment later this month. The proposed changes would permit Swiss companies to sell "war material" to a specific roster of 25 nations, primarily Western countries, even if those nations are currently engaged in a conflict. Furthermore, these recipient countries would generally be allowed to re-export the weapons to third parties. The only exclusion for deliveries would be countries judged to be systematic and severe violators of human rights.

The government will, however, maintain the authority to veto any export if it determines that the delivery endangers Swiss neutrality or core foreign and security policy interests.

While Thursday's legislative decision moves the amendment closer to a final approval, its effective date remains uncertain. Lawmakers on the political left have indicated their intent to challenge the new rules by calling for a national referendum.

The war in Ukraine put Swiss defense manufacturers under significant pressure. The country's strict neutrality-based laws prohibit the re-export of war material that is more than 50% Swiss-made. This stance led to multiple European nations barring Swiss firms from defense contracts after Switzerland blocked requests to ship ammunition and arms to Ukraine.

The volume of Swiss war material exports declined for the second consecutive year in 2024, finishing 30% lower than its peak in 2022. Consequently, several Swiss defense companies have begun moving their manufacturing operations outside the country. This shift is a direct response to avoiding the loss of profitable contracts as European states increase their defense budgets and rearm in the wake of Moscow's aggression and persistent U.S. calls for greater European security spending.

data.title: U.S. stocks struggle for direction as Meta rises on Metaverse news, less-than-expected job cuts
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were ducking underwater on Thursday, while investors assesed Meta Platorms' (META) rise after Mark Zuckerberg reportedly cutting Metaverse spending, and jobless claims came lower than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last flat in late morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.1%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.1%.

Meta Platforms (META) -- last +4.5% -- rose as Bloomberg reported that Mark Zuckerberg said that the company is looking at cutting Metaverse efforts by up to 30%.

On the economic side, the initial jobless claims retreated to below 200K and were better than expected. U.S.-based employers announced 71.32K job cuts in November, down 53% from the 153.07K announced in October.

“Initial claims collapsed last week to a three-year low. The Bureau of Labor Statistics, which compiles this report, did not note any special factors. So, as I highlighted in red all year, the less-than-5-day workweeks distort this series,” said Jim Bianco, president and macro strategist at Bianco Research.

“Last week was Thanksgiving. Previously, these short workweeks resulted in a spike in claims, but Thanksgiving week saw a collapse. Even if you adjust for this week for Thanksgiving, the data still doesn't suggest the Fed should cut rates next week,” he added.

In other news, President Donald Trump's aides were reportedly in talks about appointing Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent as the top White House economic adviser, in addition to his current role, if Kevin Hassett is picked as the next Federal Reserve chair.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.09%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.52%.
